facts.detail: Street dance starts January 22. City fiesta peaks on January 25, feast of the Conversion of St. Paul the Apostle. Activities often run from January 17 onwards.

facts.label: Where
facts.detail: Ilocos Sur, northern Luzon. Main events happen along Plaza Burgos, Plaza Salcedo, and the cobblestone Calle Crisologo.

facts.label: Patron
facts.detail: The fiesta is tied to the feast of the Conversion of St. Paul. The Metropolitan Cathedral is the seat of the Archdiocese of Nueva Segovia.

facts.label: Star Dish
facts.detail: Garlicky, slightly tangy pork sausage made with sukang Iloko vinegar. Registered as Vigan City's One Town, One Product (OTOP).

facts.label: When
facts.detail: Held annually in late April since 2016. From 1979 until 2015 it ran every three years.

facts.label: Where
facts.detail: Highland town in the Cordillera mountains of northern Luzon, around 350 kilometers north of Manila.

facts.label: Rooted in
facts.detail: Based on the old Ifugao ceremony marking a family's rise to the kadangyan (noble) class through abundant rice wine.

facts.label: Signature event
facts.detail: A 7-kilometer downhill race on handmade taltallak scooters carved from mananor and butbuta wood.

history.heading: From a kadangyan ritual to a town-wide festival
history.body:
Imbayah comes from bayah, the Ifugao word for rice wine. In old practice, a family that wanted to be recognized as kadangyan, the local noble class, had to host a long ceremony where rice wine was poured so generously that the jar was said to overflow. That image of abundance gave the rite its name and its meaning.

The public festival version was first organized in December 1979 under then Banaue Mayor Adriano Apilis Jr. The goal was practical. Tourism around the rice terraces was growing fast and elders were worried that young Ifugaos were losing the old chants, weaving patterns, and woodcarving skills. For decades after, Imbayah was held every three years.

In 2016, under Mayor Jerry U. Dalipog, the local government turned it into a yearly event so the cultural practices would be performed more often and so visitors had a reliable date to plan around. Sources: Rappler feature on Imbayah, Inquirer culture desk, and SunStar Baguio coverage of the shift to a yearly schedule.

key_events.name: Cultural Parade of the Barangays
key_events.blurb: The 12 barangays of Banaue march in five clustered groups, each in full native attire with gongs and chants.

key_events.name: Wooden Scooter Race
key_events.blurb: Around 7 kilometers of downhill mountain road on handmade taltallak scooters carved from mananor and butbuta wood.

key_events.name: Munpaot Woodcarving Competition
key_events.blurb: Carvers work live in front of the crowd, shaping bululs and other traditional pieces from a single block.

key_events.name: Rice Pounding and Bayah Sharing
key_events.blurb: Communal rice pounding followed by tasting of bayah, the fermented rice wine that gives the festival its name.

key_events.name: Boar Sacrifice Ritual
key_events.blurb: A public ritual rooted in the original kadangyan ceremony, performed by elders before the main program.

key_events.name: Mr. and Ms. Imbayah Pageant
key_events.blurb: Younger Ifugaos compete in traditional dress, with rounds on weaving, dance, and oral history.

place_intro:
Imbayah is the cultural festival of Banaue, the Ifugao highland town best known for its mountain-carved rice terraces. It is held in the last week of April, when the harvest season is winding down and the weather up in the Cordillera is dry enough for outdoor competitions and street parades. The main program runs around the town proper.

Banaue sits about 350 kilometers north of Manila, deep in the Cordillera mountains of Ifugao Province. It is a small town built around terraces that rise roughly 1,500 meters above sea level. Most travelers come in by overnight bus from Manila, then jeepney into the centro. During Imbayah week, the plaza, the municipal hall grounds, and the main road become the festival venue. Ifugao families from the surrounding barangays come down in full traditional dress, with gongs and chants moving from one street to the next.

The festival mixes ritual and competition. Some parts are sacred, like the boar sacrifice performed by elders. Other parts are loud and playful, like the wooden scooter race, the rice pounding contest, and the men's volleyball game played in traditional loincloths.
why_go:
Imbayah is one of the few large festivals in the Philippines where the entire program is built around a single indigenous group's living culture rather than a Catholic patron saint. If you have been to Sinulog or Ati-Atihan and you want to see something on the other end of the spectrum, this is it. The dances, the gongs, the chants, and the costumes all come straight from Ifugao practice, not from a choreographed contingent.

The wooden scooter race is the event most travelers plan around. Riders go down roughly 7 kilometers of mountain road on handmade taltallak scooters, carved from mananor and butbuta wood by the riders themselves or their families. It is fast, loud, and very local. Spectators line the road and the finish area gets packed, so come early if you want a clear view.

The other reason to go is the timing. Late April is dry season in the Cordillera, the terraces are still green from the standing rice, and the bus and jeepney routes from Manila and Baguio are running on normal schedules. You can pair the festival with a Batad day hike or a side trip to Sagada without rearranging the rest of your itinerary.
tip: Book your Banaue homestay or inn at least two months out. The town is small, rooms near the centro fill up first, and during Imbayah week the late April crowd includes both domestic tourists and Ifugao families coming home from Manila and Baguio. If everything in the centro is full, ask about rooms in Batad or along the road to Bangaan and ride a jeepney in.
what_unique:
What sets Imbayah apart is that it is not built on a Catholic feast day. There is no patron saint, no fluvial procession, no novena leading up to it. The festival grew out of the kadangyan rite, the old Ifugao ceremony where a family announced its rise to noble status by hosting a long feast and pouring so much rice wine that the jar overflowed. That image of abundance is still the symbol of the festival.

The costumes and tools are also specific to Ifugao, not generic tribal wear. You will see the wanno and tapis weaves, brass earrings, and rattan backpacks called pasiking. The dances are accompanied by gangsa gongs played in the seated Ifugao style, which is different from the standing Kalinga style some travelers may already know.

The wooden scooter race is the part that has become the festival's signature image. The taltallak has no engine and no metal parts in the running gear. Riders carve them from local hardwood and tune the wheels for grip on the mountain road. It is a piece of Ifugao practical engineering that exists in Banaue and the nearby towns and almost nowhere else.

facts.label: When
facts.detail: Bangus Festival runs the back half of April. Biggest crowd-pulling events land on April 26, 28, and 30.

facts.label: Where
facts.detail: Held across Dagupan City in Pangasinan, north Luzon. Major events stretch along De Venecia Highway and the Plaza.

facts.label: Famous for
facts.detail: Dagupan set the Guinness record on May 3, 2003 with a 1,007.56 meter grill cooking 10,000 bangus. The Bangusan street party recreates it yearly.

facts.label: Eat
facts.detail: Bangusine cooking contest puts boneless bangus, sisig na bangus, daing, and relleno on every block. Pair with pigar-pigar at Galvan Street.

history.heading: From a quiet balikbayan harvest to a Guinness-record street party
history.body:
Bangus Festival started small. In the early 1990s, then-mayor Al Fernandez put together a simple bangus harvest called Gilon, a welcome activity for balikbayans coming home for the summer. Gilon is the local Pangasinense word for harvesting milkfish from the ponds, and that quiet community ritual is the seed of everything you see today.

The festival was formally launched as the Dagupan City Bangus Festival in 2002 by then-mayor Benjamin Lim, timed with the city charter anniversary on April 30. A year later, on May 3, 2003, Dagupan grabbed the Guinness record for the world's longest barbecue at 1,007.56 meters, beating Peru's previous mark. That single afternoon cemented bangus as the city's identity, and the Bangusan street party has been the festival's signature event ever since.

The religious anchor is the city fiesta of San Juan Bautista, Dagupan's patron, whose feast falls in late June. The April celebration grew out of civic and economic pride in the milkfish industry rather than a saint's day, which is why it leans more food-and-culture than processional.
history.citation_label: Dagupan City Government, Manila Bulletin, Philippine Star

key_events.name: Gilon-Gilon ed Baley
key_events.blurb: Street dance competition joined by all 31 barangays of Dagupan. Choreography mimics how fishermen scoop bangus from the ponds. Held around April 26.

key_events.name: Bangus Rodeo
key_events.blurb: Contests for fastest bangus sorter, heaviest bangus, and best deboner. Loud, messy, very local. Usually April 28.

key_events.name: Bangusine Cook-Off
key_events.blurb: Restaurants and home cooks present original bangus dishes, from sisig na bangus to bangus sushi. Free tasting if you come early.

key_events.name: Kalutan ed Dalan / Bangusan Street Party
key_events.blurb: The big one on April 30. A grill that stretches more than a kilometer along De Venecia Highway, with thousands of bangus cooking at once.

key_events.name: Fun Run for Disaster Awareness
key_events.blurb: Opens the festival weeks. Loops through downtown Dagupan and doubles as a fundraiser for the city's calamity preparedness program.

key_events.name: Charter Day Parade
key_events.blurb: April 30 civic parade marking the founding of Dagupan as a chartered city, with floats from schools, barangays, and the bangus industry.

place_intro:
Bangus Festival is Dagupan City's love letter to the milkfish. The city sits on the Lingayen Gulf in Pangasinan, about four to five hours by bus from Manila, and it has built its identity around bangus farming in the brackish ponds that ring the Pantal River. Every April, the city blocks off its main roads, lights up the plaza, and turns the back half of the month into a long food and culture run.

The festival officially stretches from around April 9 to May 1, but the events most travelers come for are packed into April 17 to 30. You get a fun run, a Bangus Rodeo with sorting and deboning contests, the Gilon-Gilon ed Baley street dance, the Bangusine cooking competition, and on April 30, the Kalutan ed Dalan grill-up along De Venecia Highway. April 30 is also Dagupan's charter anniversary as a city, which is why the closing date never moves.

Dagupan itself is bigger than first-time visitors expect. It is the commercial center of western Pangasinan, with CSI malls, the Cathedral of St. John the Evangelist, Tondaligan Blue Beach, and a working river port.
why_go:
Go for the Bangusan Street Party on April 30. The visual is hard to describe until you see it: more than a thousand one-meter charcoal grills lined up along De Venecia Highway, cooking around 20,000 bangus at the same time. The smoke, the smell of grilled fish over coals, the sound of tongs clanging. This is what put Dagupan in the Guinness Book in 2003 and the city has been recreating it every year since. You can buy a slot on a grill or just walk the line and eat as you go.

The Gilon-Gilon ed Baley street dance a few days earlier is the cultural heart. All 31 barangays send a contingent, and the choreography is built around the motion of scooping bangus from ponds, weighing baskets, and hauling them to market. Costumes lean blue, silver, and aqua to mimic milkfish scales, and the audience picks favorites loudly.

For food travelers, the Bangusine cooking contest is the actual reason to come. You will see bangus done in ways you have never thought of: bangus sisig, daing na bangus with green mango, smoked bangus belly, bangus relleno stuffed with longganisa, even bangus sushi.
tip: Skip cooking your own grill on April 30. Walk the De Venecia Highway stretch around 5 pm, before the crowd thickens, and buy grilled bangus straight from a barangay slot. Bring water and wet wipes, the smoke gets thick. Park at the city plaza side and walk in, tricycles cannot pass the main road that night.
what_unique:
What sets Bangus Festival apart from the bigger Philippine fiestas is that it is not built around a saint or a religious procession. There is no Sinulog-style image, no Ati-Atihan body paint, no fluvial parade for a patron. It is a civic and food festival born from the city's identity as the country's bangus capital, and that gives it a different rhythm. The center of gravity is a kilometer of grills, not an altar.

The Gilon-Gilon ed Baley dance is also distinct because it is choreographed around fishery labor instead of a battle or a courtship. The movements come from actual bangus harvesting, the scooping motion, the sorting, the weighing, even the haggling at market. Costume designers in each barangay study scale patterns and pond colors, so the parade looks more like a moving aquarium than a typical fiesta line-up.

The other unique piece is the timing with the city charter on April 30. Most Philippine fiestas lock onto a saint's feast day, but Dagupan deliberately fused its civic anniversary with the festival climax. That is why the Bangusan Street Party feels less like a religious closing and more like a citywide birthday cookout.

facts.label: When
facts.detail: Main festival day coincides with Labor Day. Activities run through April with the provincial government lining up events almost daily until May 1.

facts.label: Where
facts.detail: Centered on Lingayen Beach and the provincial capitol grounds along Lingayen Gulf. The Banca Parada fluvial parade runs along the Agno River.

facts.label: Meaning
facts.detail: Pista is from Spanish fiesta. Dayat means sea in Pangasinense. The festival is a community thanksgiving for the bounty of Lingayen Gulf.

facts.label: Highlight
facts.detail: Hundreds of decorated fishing boats line the Agno River in a grand fluvial parade, with crews dressing up their boats to depict local legends and folklore.

history.heading: Roots in postwar fishing villages of Lingayen Gulf
history.body: Pista'y Dayat traces its formal beginnings to 1970, though elders in Pangasinan say the practice had already been alive through the 1960s. In the small fishing towns along Lingayen Gulf, especially Lingayen and Alaminos, fisherfolk would gather every first of May to give thanks for the sea's bounty. They would wade into the seawater on that one day, holding onto the belief that the sea could heal ailments of all kinds if you dipped in it on the first of May. As Lingayen and Alaminos started trading more actively with each other, the small village celebrations grew bigger and eventually got picked up by the provincial government. Today the festival keeps that fishing-community core: local fisherfolk still open each Pista'y Dayat with bamboo raft offerings, a quiet ritual that honors the sea's role in sustaining the more than 62,000 fishing families across Pangasinan.

key_events.name: Banca Parada Fluvial Parade
key_events.blurb: Hundreds of fishing boats decorated by their crews float down the Agno River in a grand parade, with some boats acting out local legends like Limahong's 1574 invasion.

key_events.name: Beach Mass and Bamboo Raft Offering
key_events.blurb: Local fisherfolk open the festival with a mass on Lingayen Beach and a bamboo raft offering to the sea, the most solemn part of the week.

key_events.name: Limgas na Pangasinan
key_events.blurb: The province's signature beauty pageant where candidates from different towns compete, one of the most attended nights of the festival.

key_events.name: Sand Sculpting and Bangus Art Exhibit
key_events.blurb: Teams build sand sculptures on Lingayen Beach while a separate exhibit features artwork themed around bangus, the province's prized milkfish.

key_events.name: Trade, Tourism, Food, and Garden Fair
key_events.blurb: Booths set up at the capitol grounds showcase Pangasinan products, bangus dishes, sweets, and crafts from the province's coastal towns.

key_events.name: Lingayen Gulf Coastal Clean-up
key_events.blurb: Usually held on the last day, volunteers and government workers clean the shoreline along the capitol, closing the festival on a stewardship note.

place_intro:
Pista'y Dayat, the Sea Festival, is Pangasinan's biggest thanksgiving celebration to Lingayen Gulf, the body of water that has fed the province for generations. The main day falls on May 1, which lines up with Labor Day, but the provincial government in Lingayen lines up activities almost daily from early April until then. The festival is centered on the capital town of Lingayen, with most of the action on Lingayen Beach and at the provincial capitol grounds across the road.

Lingayen itself is the capital of Pangasinan and sits right on the shore of Lingayen Gulf. History buffs know it as the site where General Douglas MacArthur and the Allied forces landed on January 9, 1945 to begin the liberation of Luzon. The town was founded in 1614, and its name comes from the Pangasinan word lingawen, meaning to look back.

On the ground, the week feels like a fishing-village fiesta that grew into a provincial event. Expect a fluvial parade on the Agno River, a beach mass, food fairs, beauty pageants, sand sculpting, and a coastal clean-up before everyone goes home.
why_go:
Go for the Banca Parada. This is the moment locals come out for: hundreds of fishing boats, each decorated by its own crew, gliding down the Agno River. Some boats take it seriously and act out historical episodes like Limahong's 1574 invasion of Pangasinan. You can watch from the riverbanks for free, and the closer you are to the take-off point, the better the photos.

Go for the food. Pangasinan is bangus country, and during Pista'y Dayat the Trade, Tourism, Food, and Garden Fair at the capitol turns into a parade of milkfish in every form, plus tupig, puto Calasiao, and bagoong from the coastal towns. The fair runs for most of the festival, so even if you only catch one day you will still eat well. The beach mass and bamboo raft offering on the opening morning are also worth waking up early for if you want to see the quieter, more solemn side of the festival.

Go because it is easy to combine with a beach trip. Lingayen Beach itself is right there, and Hundred Islands in Alaminos is about an hour and a half away by van. You can plan the trip so you catch the May 1 main day, then move on to the islands the day after, which is what most Manila-based families do.
what_unique:
What sets Pista'y Dayat apart from most Philippine fiestas is that it is not a saint's day. It is a thanksgiving to the sea itself. There is no patron whose image is paraded through town. The center of devotion is Lingayen Gulf, and the symbolic act is the bamboo raft offering that local fisherfolk float out at the opening. The dipping tradition is also distinct: elders believed that on the first of May, the seawater of Lingayen Gulf could heal ailments of all sorts, only on that one day of the year. You will still see families wading in for that reason.

The festival is also unusual in scale because it is run by the provincial government, not a single barangay or parish. That means the program is bigger than a typical town fiesta, with Limgas na Pangasinan, an orchestra showdown, dancesports, and a solo vocal contest all at the capitol. At the same time, the village rituals are still intact, so you get two layers in one week.

The third distinct piece is the Banca Parada. Most Philippine fluvial processions carry a religious image down a river. Here, the river is the stage and the fishermen are the ones being honored, with boats freely decorated in local themes.
tip: Stay in Dagupan instead of Lingayen if you book late. Dagupan has more hotels and is only about 20 to 30 minutes away by tricycle or jeep, and you will still make the early-morning beach mass on Lingayen Beach. Book your ride back the same night because public transport thins out fast after the May 1 program closes.

facts.label: When
facts.detail: Month-long celebration with the Sandugo sa Dalan street dancing usually on the 3rd or 4th Sunday of July.

facts.label: Where
facts.detail: Main events run along the city's central avenues and end at the Carlos P. Garcia Sports Complex in Bohol.

facts.label: Headline event
facts.detail: A live restaging of the 1565 Sikatuna and Legazpi pact that gave the festival its name, meaning 'one blood' in Visayan.

facts.label: First held
facts.detail: Launched under then Governor Constancio Torralba and has grown into one of Visayas' biggest civic and historical festivals.

history.heading: From a 1565 pact to a city-wide July tradition
history.body: The Sandugo Festival traces its name to the Kasikas sa Sandugo, the blood compact performed on Bohol soil on March 16, 1565, between Datu Sikatuna, a local chieftain, and the Spanish explorer Miguel Lopez de Legazpi. Following Visayan custom, both men cut their arms, mixed their blood with wine, and drank from the same cup to seal a pact of friendship. Filipino history textbooks remember it as the first recorded treaty of friendship between a native ruler and a European in the country, and the word sandugo itself simply means 'one blood' in Visayan. The provincial capital, Tagbilaran City, later turned that moment into a civic point of pride. The first Sandugo Festival was staged in 1989 during the term of then Bohol Governor Constancio Torralba as a way to commemorate the pact and pull the province's tourism, schools, and barangays into one shared July program. What started as a short commemoration has since expanded into a month-long calendar of street dancing, trade fairs, beauty pageants, and a reenactment of the 1565 ritual itself, performed every year in front of crowds at the sports complex.

key_events.name: Charter Day Kickoff
key_events.blurb: July 1 opens the festival with a holy mass, diana, motorcade, and civic program led by Tagbilaran City Hall.

key_events.name: Sandugo sa Dalan
key_events.blurb: Street dancing competition usually held on the 3rd or 4th Sunday of July, running from the City Pier through the main avenues.

key_events.name: Blood Compact Reenactment
key_events.blurb: A live restaging of the 1565 Sikatuna and Legazpi ritual, performed at the Carlos P. Garcia Sports Complex after the parade.

key_events.name: Miss Bohol Sandugo Pageant
key_events.blurb: One of the most coveted titles in the province, with the winners later riding the lead float of the parade.

key_events.name: Grand Float Parade
key_events.blurb: Decorated floats from city barangays, municipalities, and schools wind through the central streets ahead of the dance finals.

key_events.name: Trade Fair and Nightly Shows
key_events.blurb: Daily booths from Bohol towns sell native delicacies, woven goods, and pasalubong, with free open-air entertainment most nights.

place_intro: Sandugo Festival is a month-long historical and civic celebration held every July in Tagbilaran City, the capital of Bohol. It commemorates the Kasikas sa Sandugo, the blood compact performed on March 16, 1565 between Datu Sikatuna of Bohol and Spanish explorer Miguel Lopez de Legazpi. The word sandugo is Visayan for 'one blood,' which is the core idea the city celebrates each year: friendship sealed between two peoples through a shared ritual.\n\nTagbilaran City is the gateway to the rest of Bohol, including the Chocolate Hills, Panglao Island, and the old stone churches of Loboc and Baclayon. During Sandugo month, the city pier, the rotunda, and the streets leading to the Carlos P. Garcia Sports Complex become the main stage. Local high schools, barangays, and guest contingents from other Bohol towns take over the avenues for daytime parades, while evenings move into trade fair booths and free open-air shows.
why_go:
Go for the parade weekend. Sandugo sa Dalan is the single biggest day of the festival, with contingents from Tagbilaran high schools and guest groups from other Bohol municipalities performing full choreographed routines in costume. The route starts around the City Pier, passes through the main streets, and ends at the Carlos P. Garcia Sports Complex, where the Blood Compact Reenactment is staged in front of the crowd. Seeing the actual restaging of the 1565 pact, costumes, daggers, wine cup and all, on the same island where it happened is the part most first-timers come for.

Go for the Miss Bohol Sandugo pageant if you like Filipino beauty pageants done at provincial scale. It is one of the most followed titles in the province, and the winners traditionally ride the lead float the following weekend. Nightly programs around the plaza also pull in local bands and folk dance groups, so even on non-parade nights there is usually something free to watch.

Go for the trade fair if you want pasalubong without doing a town-by-town road trip. Booths from Bohol municipalities sell calamay, peanut kisses, broas, ube, and woven raffia bags in one place.
what_unique:
What sets Sandugo apart from other big Philippine festivals is that it is built around a specific dated historical event, not a patron saint or a harvest. Ati-Atihan, Sinulog, and Dinagyang are religious in root. Sandugo is civic and historical, anchored to March 16, 1565 and the blood compact between Sikatuna and Legazpi. The reenactment is treated as the main act, not a side ritual, and is performed by Boholano actors at the sports complex every year in front of a full crowd.

The other distinct thing is the meaning of the word itself. Sandugo is Visayan for 'one blood,' and the program leans into that idea of kinship: between Spain and Bohol historically, and today between Tagbilaran and the other Bohol towns that send guest contingents. You will see town names you would normally only meet on a countryside tour, like Loboc, Loon, Maribojoc, and Baclayon, marching together in one parade.

It is also a month-long festival rather than a single-week burst. That gives DIY travelers flexibility. You do not need to land on one specific day; trade fairs, nightly shows, and pageant rounds run across the whole of July, with the parade and reenactment as the peak.
tip: Book your Tagbilaran or Panglao accommodation at least 2 to 3 months out if you are targeting the street dancing weekend. Rooms inside the city fill up fast and rates climb the closer you get. If city hotels are full, stay in Panglao and ride a multicab or grab into Tagbilaran on parade day, then leave before the late-night fireworks crowd hits the bridge.

facts.label: When
facts.detail: Two-day fiesta honoring San Clemente, patron saint of fishermen, held every November in Angono.

facts.label: Where
facts.detail: Lakeshore town about 30 km east of Manila, reachable by jeep or UV Express from Cubao or Pasig.

facts.label: Signature sight
facts.detail: Papier-mache higantes parade through town, each barangay sending its own towering figure with a story to tell.

facts.label: Pair it with
facts.detail: Angono is the hometown of Botong Francisco and Lucio San Pedro. Slot in a visit to local galleries and the Petroglyphs.

history.heading: From Spanish-era protest to papier-mache parade
history.body: Local folklore traces the higantes back to the Spanish colonial period, when Angono was a hacienda town and residents were allowed only one town fiesta a year. The story goes that townsfolk built oversized papier-mache figures as a quiet form of mockery against the hacienderos who limited their celebrations, exaggerating the look of stern landlords with their hands on hips and stiff postures. The figures grew into a tradition tied to the feast of San Clemente, the patron saint of Angono and of fishermen, whose image is paraded on a pagoda along Laguna de Bay during the fluvial procession. Over time the higantes shed their pure satire and became proud icons of the barangays, each one representing a family, trade, or community story. The Department of Tourism and the Angono local government now promote the November fiesta as one of the headline cultural events in CALABARZON, with Angono itself recognized as the Art Capital of the Philippines for its long line of National Artists.
history.citation_label: Department of Tourism / Angono LGU cultural briefs on Higantes Festival

key_events.name: Higantes Parade
key_events.blurb: Dozens of papier-mache giants, each around ten feet tall, dance through the main roads with their barangay contingents and brass bands.

key_events.name: Fluvial Procession of San Clemente
key_events.blurb: The saint is brought out on a decorated pagoda along Laguna de Bay while devotees on bancas splash water in blessing.

key_events.name: Basaan
key_events.blurb: Crowds along the procession route get drenched on purpose. Locals throw water from buckets, hoses, and water guns as part of the fisherman tradition.

key_events.name: Pagoda Float Competition
key_events.blurb: Barangays decorate boats and floats around fishing and faith themes, judged for craftsmanship and story.

key_events.name: Sayawan sa Kalye
key_events.blurb: Street dancing troupes in costume compete in front of the municipal hall, with judging that draws big hometown crowds.

key_events.name: Art Crawl Sidetrip
key_events.blurb: Galleries of Nemiranda, Blanco Family Museum, and the studios along Doña Aurora open their doors during the fiesta weekend.

place_intro:
Higantes Festival is the November town fiesta of Angono, Rizal, held every 22 and 23 in honor of San Clemente, the patron saint of fishermen. The whole town wakes up to brass bands, the smell of frying tilapia, and the sight of giant papier-mache figures called higantes lining the parish road. Each barangay sends at least one higante, ten feet tall on average, carried by a person inside the hollow body.

Angono sits on the western shore of Laguna de Bay, around thirty kilometers east of Manila. It is a small lakeside town, easy to walk around in a day, and famous beyond the fiesta for being the Art Capital of the Philippines, the hometown of National Artists Carlos Botong Francisco and Lucio San Pedro. That artistic blood runs through the festival itself. The higantes are not factory-made props but yearly community art pieces.

For a DIY traveler from Metro Manila, the festival is a clean one-day or overnight trip. The town center is compact, the action is free to watch, and food stalls open early along the main streets so you can graze your way through the parade route."
tip: Wear quick-dry clothes and seal your phone in a zip-lock. The basaan is not optional once you stand along the procession route, locals will douse you with buckets, hoses, and water guns, and dodging it is considered bad form. Park outside the town proper near Angono church and walk in. The narrow streets get closed off by mid-morning.
what_unique:
No other Philippine fiesta uses giants on this scale. While other towns parade saints, floats, or higantes-style puppets in smaller numbers, Angono fields dozens at once, each one a barangay project carried on the shoulders of a single person. The result is a moving forest of stiff-armed, big-headed figures bobbing above the crowd, every one of them with a back story tied to the family or trade that sponsored it.

The pairing with the basaan and the fluvial procession is also specific to Angono. The water soaking is not just festival mischief, it ties back to the fisherman identity of the town and the blessing of San Clemente over Laguna de Bay. You get the dry land spectacle of giants in the morning and the lakeside boat procession in the afternoon, all in one fiesta.

The art-town backdrop changes how the festival feels. Galleries along the main road stay open, muralists set up live work near the plaza, and the higantes themselves are sculpted by local artists who treat them as yearly commissions. You are not watching a tourism office production. You are watching a town of artists throw their own party."
why_go:
Go if you want a Philippine fiesta that is loud, visual, and unmistakably local. The higantes are the headliner and they deliver. Standing at the corner of the parish road and watching ten or fifteen of them sway down the street at once, with the brass bands behind them and kids running underneath, is the kind of scene a video clip cannot fully carry. You have to be in it.

Go for the basaan, the water-drenching that runs alongside the procession of San Clemente. It is the Angono version of letting the saint bless the town and its fishermen, and the whole route turns into a friendly soaking match between barangays and visitors. Bring a dry bag. Bring nothing you cannot wash. You will leave wet and happy.

Go also for the art. Angono is a working town of painters, sculptors, and musicians. The fiesta weekend is when most of the galleries, ancestral houses, and studios are open without an appointment, so you can pair the festival with a slow afternoon at the Blanco Family Museum, Nemiranda Arthouse, or the Angono Petroglyphs nearby. Few fiestas in Luzon let you mix folk Catholic ritual, street parade, and a serious art crawl in the same day."

facts.label: Main day
facts.detail: Feast of San Juan Bautista. The lechon parade starts late morning, then the basaan continues into the afternoon.

facts.label: Where
facts.detail: Western Batangas town, around 110 km south of Manila. The parade loops through the main streets around the church plaza.

facts.label: Expect to get soaked
facts.detail: Locals hose down everyone with tabo, timba, and water guns. Bring a dry bag, waterproof phone pouch, and a change of clothes.

facts.label: Pigs in costume
facts.detail: Whole roasted pigs are dressed in raincoats, sunglasses, jerseys, even bridal gowns, and paraded on trucks, tricycles, and bamboo poles.

history.heading: From a Kanluran district thanksgiving custom to a town-wide feast
history.body:
The Parada ng Lechon traces back to a thanksgiving practice among working-class families in the Kanluran (western) district of Balayan. During the Spanish and American colonial periods, families who had a good harvest, a safe sea voyage, or any answered prayer would roast a whole pig and parade it through the streets every June 24, the feast day of San Juan Bautista, the town patron. The lechon was both an offering of thanks and a public act of devotion.

The practice first appears in written records in 1906, when local accounts describe the lechon parade as a way to honor San Juan Bautista. As more families in Kanluran prospered through the early 20th century, more pigs joined the procession, and what was once a humble district custom grew into a town-wide spectacle.

In 1959, residents formally organized the Hermandad San Juan Bautista, the Brotherhood of San Juan Bautista, to manage the celebration. Under the brotherhood, the single-day feast slowly expanded into a multi-day affair with sports, pageants, and street events, while the morning Mass, the procession of the saint, and the luwa (religious declamation) kept the religious core intact.
history.citation_label: Parada ng Lechon in Balayan: Ethnographic Study (PH EJournals)

key_events.name: Holy Mass at the Parish Church
key_events.blurb: The day opens with a morning Mass at the centuries-old Immaculate Conception Parish, the spiritual anchor of the feast for San Juan Bautista.

key_events.name: Parada ng Lechon proper
key_events.blurb: Dozens of roasted pigs ride on trucks, tricycles, and shoulder-carried bamboo poles, many dressed in raincoats, costumes, and sunglasses to protect them from the basaan.

key_events.name: Basaan sa Kalye
key_events.blurb: Residents along the route soak the crowd with hoses, tabo, timba, and water guns, echoing the baptism by San Juan Bautista of Christ in the Jordan.

key_events.name: Luwa (religious declamation)
key_events.blurb: Young residents recite traditional verses in honor of San Juan Bautista, a quieter ritual that survives alongside the wet street party.

key_events.name: Salu-salo and lechon feast
key_events.blurb: After the parade, families and visitors gather to carve and share the parade lechons, with each pig usually returning to the host family for a community meal.

key_events.name: Fiesta-week side events
key_events.blurb: The Hermandad usually adds a beauty pageant, sports tournaments, a battle of the bands, and nightly free concerts in the days leading up to June 24.

place_intro:
Parada ng Lechon is Balayan, Batangas' loudest, wettest, and most photographed fiesta. Every June 24, families and barangays parade whole roasted pigs through the town streets to honor San Juan Bautista, the town patron, while residents soak the entire crowd with the traditional basaan, or water dousing. It is a fiesta where faith, food, and a full-on water fight share the same kalye.

Balayan itself is a coastal town in western Batangas, about 110 km south of Manila and roughly two and a half hours by bus or car. It is a working fishing port best known for being the cradle of Philippine bagoong (fermented fish paste) and, of course, for this feast. On a normal day Balayan is a quiet provincial town. On June 24, the population doubles with returning families, food vendors, and visitors from Manila, Tagaytay, Batangas City, and overseas.

The feast is anchored at the Immaculate Conception Parish Church near the town plaza, with the parade looping through the main streets around it. Side streets host food stalls selling lechon by the kilo, sinaing na tulingan, bulalo, and the famous Balayan bagoong.
tip: Wear quick-dry clothes, sandals with a strap, and a swim shirt if you sunburn easily. Put your phone, cash, and IDs in a waterproof pouch around your neck, because basaan is non-negotiable along the parade route. Stake out a spot near the plaza by 9 AM before the main street closes.
what_unique:
No other Philippine fiesta puts whole roasted pigs in costume and walks them down the street. Each barangay, family, or sponsor commissions a lechon, then dresses it up for the parade. Past years have featured pigs in raincoats, basketball jerseys, Barong Tagalog, wedding gowns, sunglasses, and even mini sun umbrellas, partly as humor and partly to protect the crispy skin from the water dousing.

The basaan layer is what truly sets Balayan apart. While many Philippine towns hold a lechon parade or a santo procession, only Balayan pairs it with a town-wide water fight rooted in the baptism story of San Juan Bautista. Fire trucks hose down the crowd in front of the church, residents pour buckets from rooftops, and kids run the sidelines with water guns.

Layered under the spectacle is the older luwa tradition, where residents recite religious verses in honor of San Juan Bautista. Combined with the Mass, the Hermandad-led procession, and the post-parade salu-salo, this is one of the few fiestas where faith, folk ritual, and street humor run side by side in the same afternoon.
why_go:
Parada ng Lechon is the easiest "wow" fiesta to reach from Manila. You can leave EDSA in the early morning, watch the full parade and basaan by noon, eat your fill of fresh lechon, then be back in the city by night if you skip the after-party. Few major Philippine fiestas are this accessible in a single day trip.

The lechon itself is the other reason. Balayan and the wider Batangas region are known for native pig, slow-roasted over coals, with skin that snaps. On June 24, the streets are lined with that exact smell, and after the parade many of the display pigs are sliced up and shared, either by the host family or sold by the kilo to visitors.

Third, the basaan is genuinely fun if you go in dressed for it. The water dousing breaks down the usual tourist-spectator wall. Once you are wet, you are part of the fiesta, not just watching it, and Balayanenos are famously warm hosts to anyone who plays along.

facts.label: When
facts.detail: The Fluvial Procession anchors the festival on the third Saturday, with the Traslacion happening on the second Friday of September the week prior.

facts.label: Where
facts.detail: Centered on the Basilica Minore of Our Lady of Penafrancia, the Naga Metropolitan Cathedral, and the Naga River in the Bicol Region.

facts.label: Crowd Size
facts.detail: Considered the biggest Marian devotion in Asia, drawing devotees from across the Bicol Region and the Filipino diaspora.

facts.label: Patroness
facts.detail: The image was canonically crowned Patroness of Bicolandia on September 20, 1924, by Apostolic Delegate Most Rev. Guglielmo Piani.

history.heading: From a Spanish mountain shrine to the Naga River
history.body:
The devotion traces back to Pena de Francia (Rock of France) in Salamanca, Spain, where a hermit named Simon Vela is said to have discovered a hidden Marian image in the 1400s after a series of visions. The cult of Our Lady of Penafrancia crossed the Pacific in the early 18th century through Miguel Robles de Covarrubias, a young seminarian in Nueva Caceres (now Naga). Records from the Archdiocese of Caceres note that in 1710, Covarrubias fell gravely ill and prayed to the Virgin of Pena de Francia for healing. When he recovered, he commissioned a local artisan to carve a replica of the Spanish image and built a chapel along the banks of the Naga River, the site of today's Basilica Minore.

The festival itself took its modern shape in 1864, when Bishop Francisco Gainza of Nueva Caceres formalized two now-iconic rites: the Traslacion, which transfers the image from the Basilica to the Naga Metropolitan Cathedral for the novena, and the Fluvial Procession, which returns the image by river barge to the Basilica. On September 20, 1924, Apostolic Delegate Most Rev. Guglielmo Piani canonically crowned the image as Queen and Patroness of Bicolandia. The traditions have run for more than 160 years and now draw over a million devotees, making it the largest Marian gathering in Asia.
history.citation_label: Archdiocese of Caceres: History of the Devotion

key_events.name: Traslacion Procession
key_events.blurb: Held on the second Friday of September, barefoot male devotees called voyadores carry Ina's image from the Basilica to the Naga Metropolitan Cathedral, chanting 'Viva la Virgen!'

key_events.name: Nine-Day Novena
key_events.blurb: From the day after the Traslacion through the eve of the Fluvial Procession, daily Masses run at the Naga Cathedral with packed crowds praying for intercession and thanksgiving.

key_events.name: Fluvial Procession
key_events.blurb: The festival's climax on the third Saturday: Ina's image is returned to the Basilica aboard a decorated pagoda barge that floats down the Naga River, escorted by hundreds of bancas and voyadores.

key_events.name: Military Civic Parade
key_events.blurb: Held on Fluvial morning before the river rites, with marching bands, AFP units, schools, and Bicol LGUs filling Magsaysay Avenue from sunrise.

key_events.name: Voyadores Festival Street Dance
key_events.blurb: A street dance and showdown along the downtown route where contingents from Camarines Sur towns interpret the Marian devotion through Bicolano choreography and costume.

key_events.name: Miss Bicolandia Pageant
key_events.blurb: The regional beauty tilt held at the Naga City Coliseum during festival week, where candidates from the six Bicol provinces compete for the crown.

place_intro:
The Penafrancia Festival is the yearly devotion to Nuestra Senora de Penafrancia, the canonically crowned Queen and Patroness of Bicolandia, and it is the biggest Marian gathering in Asia. Bicolanos simply call her 'Ina' (Mother), and for nine days every September, Naga City turns into one long act of thanksgiving, with novena Masses, processions, river rites, and street dancing layered on top of the everyday hustle of the regional capital.

The festival is hosted by Naga City in Camarines Sur, the religious and commercial center of the Bicol Region. The two anchor sites are the Basilica Minore of Our Lady of Penafrancia, where the original 1710 image is enshrined, and the Naga Metropolitan Cathedral, where Ina stays for the duration of the novena. The Naga River connecting the two is the stage for the festival's climax, the Fluvial Procession, held on the third Saturday of September.

The religious core is solemn, but the surrounding week is full-on Bicolano fiesta: streetside grills, sili-laced Bicol express in the carinderias, marching bands cutting through Magsaysay Avenue, and balikbayan families who time flights home around the September weekend."
tip: Book your Naga accommodation by July at the latest. Hotels and homestays from Naga to Pili and Magarao fill up months ahead of the third Saturday, and walk-in rates spike. If you are coming for the Fluvial, stake out a spot along the Naga River by 2 PM, bring a payong and a tumbler of water, and wear closed shoes. The crowd push from the voyadores is real.
what_unique:
No other Philippine fiesta has the Fluvial Procession. On the third Saturday of September, Ina's image is loaded onto a pagoda barge and floated down the Naga River from the Cathedral back to the Basilica, with the bridges and riverbanks packed shoulder-to-shoulder. Hundreds of smaller bancas escort the pagoda, all-male voyadores in maroon and yellow wade and tow, and the chant of 'Viva la Virgen!' rolls down the river for hours. It is loud, sweaty, and unmistakably Bicolano.

The festival is also unusual in being two feasts in one week. The Divino Rostro (the Holy Face of Jesus) is honored on the second Friday of September, and Our Lady of Penafrancia is honored on the third weekend. Both images travel together in the Traslacion and the Fluvial Procession, which is why locals describe the festival as 'magkasamang inaararuga' (raised together).

Layered on top is the secular Penafrancia Festival arm run by Naga City: the Voyadores street dance, the Miss Bicolandia pageant, military civic parade, agro-trade fair, and the late-night street parties around Plaza Quezon. Few fiestas in the country fuse 300-year-old Marian devotion with a full civic carnival this tightly."
why_go:
For Catholic Filipinos, attending the Penafrancia at least once is a bucket-list act of devotion. The energy of a million voyadores chanting 'Viva la Virgen, Viva la Virgen!' as the pagoda barge moves down the Naga River is something the photos never quite capture. Even non-devotees describe the Fluvial Procession as one of the most powerful crowd experiences they have ever joined in the Philippines.

Go for the Bicolano food run, too. September in Naga is when every carinderia, panciteria, and roadside grill is in fiesta mode. This is the place to eat real Bicol express, laing, kinunot na pating, tinutungang manok, and the regional pansit bato, plus the pili tarts and pili brittle to take home. Restaurants along Magsaysay Avenue and inside SM Naga run extended hours through the novena week.

Finally, the festival is a clean way to base yourself in Naga and side-trip through Camarines Sur. Mt. Isarog, the wakeboarding at CWC, the heritage churches of the Caceres archdiocese, and the Caramoan island circuit are all within day-trip range, so you can pair Friday's Traslacion and Saturday's Fluvial with a Sunday on the water."

facts.label: When
facts.detail: Province-wide programming across Albay, with the main events clustered in Legazpi City. Around 80 activities are spread across the month.

facts.label: Where
facts.detail: Capitol grounds, Penaranda Park, Legazpi Boulevard, and Albay Astrodome serve as the main hubs, with satellite events in towns around Mayon Volcano.

facts.label: Founded
facts.detail: Started in 1995 and made an official province-wide annual event in 1998 under Governor Al Francis Bichara.

facts.label: Theme
facts.detail: Named after the Bicol word magayon (beautiful) and the legend of Daragang Magayon, the maiden whose story explains the rise of Mount Mayon.

history.heading: From a tourism push in the 90s to Albay's flagship celebration
history.body:
The Magayon Festival was launched in 1995 as a local tourism initiative and was formally established as a yearly province-wide event in 1998 during the term of then-Governor Al Francis Bichara. The goal was to give Albay a single banner celebration that could showcase its agriculture, food, crafts, sports, and cultural traditions to both Filipino and foreign travelers, with Mount Mayon as the backdrop.

In 2007 the provincial government merged it with Legazpi City's Ibalong Festival under the joint title Ibalong Na, Magayon Pa, but the arrangement only lasted one cycle. By 2008 the province brought back Magayon as a stand-alone festival running the whole month of May. The name itself is rooted in the legend of Daragang Magayon and her lover Panganoron, whose tragic story is the folk explanation for how Mount Mayon came to be.

Today the festival is coordinated by the Provincial Tourism, Culture and Arts Office of Albay, with the City of Legazpi hosting most of the headline programs. It has grown into one of the bigger May festivals in Luzon, with around 80 separate activities folded under the Magayon banner each year.

key_events.name: Marian Fluvial Parade
key_events.blurb: Traditional opening on May 1 along Albay Gulf, with the image of Our Lady of Salvacion carried on a flower-decked boat past the Legazpi shoreline.

key_events.name: Mutya ng Daragang Magayon
key_events.blurb: The flagship pageant searching for the festival muse, named after the legendary maiden of the Mayon legend. Held over several gala nights inside the Albay Astrodome.

key_events.name: Street Dance and Higantes Parade
key_events.blurb: Contingents from Albay towns dance through Penaranda Street in Bicolano costumes, joined by giant paper-mache higantes drawn from local folklore.

key_events.name: Albay Fiesta Culinaria
key_events.blurb: Parade of Chefs and cook-fest featuring Bicol Express, laing, pinangat, and other coconut milk and chili dishes prepared by Albayano chefs and home cooks.

key_events.name: Agri-Trade and Crafts Fair
key_events.blurb: Province-wide booths at the Capitol grounds selling pili nut products, abaca crafts, sili-based pasalubong, and farm produce from the towns ringing Mayon.

key_events.name: Sports and Adventure Events
key_events.blurb: Side tournaments and outdoor races that lean on Legazpi's volcano playground, from fun runs along the boulevard to ATV and biking events near Cagsawa Ruins.

place_intro:
Magayon Festival is the month-long flagship celebration of the Province of Albay, with most of the headline programs running in Legazpi City throughout the whole of May. The word magayon means beautiful in Bicol, and the festival takes its name and theme from Daragang Magayon, the legendary maiden whose story is the folk origin of Mount Mayon. The volcano sits in the background of nearly every event, which is part of why locals call this the celebration of the beauty of Albay.

Legazpi City is the capital of Albay and the gateway into the Bicol region, sitting at the foot of Mayon along Albay Gulf. For the festival the provincial government turns the Capitol grounds, Penaranda Park, Legazpi Boulevard, and the Albay Astrodome into venues for parades, pageants, food fairs, and trade shows. Surrounding towns like Daraga, Camalig, and Tabaco also host their own satellite programs under the Magayon banner.

For DIY travelers the appeal is simple. You get one base in Legazpi, easy jeepney and tricycle access to the venues, and a whole month of overlapping cultural, food, and sports events to choose from, all framed by one of the most photographed mountains in the country.
tip: Base yourself in Legazpi City near Penaranda Park or the boulevard so you can walk to most opening-week events, then rent a tricycle or motorbike on the second week to chase the satellite programs in Daraga and Camalig. Book your room before mid-April since the pageant nights and the May 1 fluvial parade fill up downtown hotels fast.
what_unique:
What sets Magayon apart from other Philippine festivals is that it is not pegged to a single fiesta day or a single patron. It is a province-wide programming calendar that stretches across all four to five weeks of May, with around 80 separate events folded under one name. You can pick the days that match your interests, whether that is food, pageantry, sports, or trade fairs.

The second distinct feature is the Mount Mayon framing. Almost every parade route, photo op, and pageant stage is positioned so that the near perfect cone of Mayon is part of the backdrop. The festival's storyline, costumes, and the Mutya ng Daragang Magayon pageant all draw directly from the Bicolano legend of Magayon and Panganoron, so the volcano is treated as a character in the celebration, not just scenery.

Finally, Magayon leans hard into Bicolano cuisine. The Albay Fiesta Culinaria turns the city into a parade of chefs serving laing, pinangat, Bicol Express, and pili-based sweets, which makes the festival as much an eating trip as a cultural one.
why_go:
Come to Magayon if you want one trip that delivers a full sampler of Bicol culture. Within a single week in Legazpi you can catch a street dance competition, a Higantes parade, a cooking show featuring laing and Bicol Express, an agri-trade fair stacked with pili nut products, and a beauty pageant gala, all without leaving the city center.

The festival is also a strong excuse to combine cultural travel with outdoor side trips. May is the tail end of the dry season in Albay, so Mayon viewing days at Cagsawa Ruins and Lignon Hill are usually clear, and the boulevard sunset is reliable. You can plan ATV rides, island hops to the Sula Channel, and Donsol whale-shark interactions on non-event days and slot the parades and pageant nights around them.

For budget DIY travelers, the lineup is mostly free. Street dancing, parades, the agri-trade fair, and the fluvial parade do not charge entrance, and only the pageant gala nights at the Astrodome usually have ticketed seats. That makes it one of the more wallet-friendly major festivals in the country if you plan around the free events.

facts.label: When
facts.detail: Main events typically peak around August 14 to 16, with month-long activities running the whole of August.

facts.label: Where
facts.detail: Most events center on People's Park, Magsaysay Park, San Pedro Street, and Roxas Avenue in downtown Davao.

facts.label: Main Highlights
facts.detail: Indak-Indak sa Kadalanan street dance and the Pamulak sa Kadayawan floral float parade are the two crowd-drawing days.

facts.label: Founded
facts.detail: Officially named Kadayawan in 1988 by then-Mayor Rodrigo Duterte, building on the 1986 Apo Duwaling Festival and earlier 1970s tribal thanksgiving.

history.heading: From tribal thanksgiving to Davao's biggest cultural week
history.body:
The roots of Kadayawan go back generations before it had a name. The eleven ethnolinguistic tribes of Davao, the Lumad groups (Ata, Matigsalug, Ovu-Manuvu, Tagabawa, Klata, Bagobo) and the Moro peoples (Tausug, Maguindanao, Maranao, Iranun, Sama), would gather after harvest to thank their supreme being Manama for a good yield, laying out fruits, rice, and vegetables in communal rituals.

In 1970, then-Mayor Elias B. Lopez, himself of Bagobo descent, encouraged the tribes to showcase those thanksgiving rituals publicly. In 1986, in the unsettled aftermath of martial law, the city formalized the celebration as the Apo Duwaling Festival, a name that smashed together Mount Apo, the durian fruit, and the waling-waling orchid as symbols of Davao pride. In 1988, Mayor Rodrigo Duterte renamed it Kadayawan sa Dabaw, drawn from the Mandaya word madayaw, meaning good, beautiful, or treasured. From 2019 onward, the celebration officially stretched across the whole month of August, with the third-week peak still holding as the main event.
history.citation_label: Davao City Government and Sunstar Davao

key_events.name: Indak-Indak sa Kadalanan
key_events.blurb: The headline street dance competition where contingents in tribal regalia perform along Roxas Avenue and San Pedro Street.

key_events.name: Pamulak sa Kadayawan
key_events.blurb: The floral float parade where trucks are wrapped in fresh blooms, fruits, and woven tribal crafts from across Mindanao.

key_events.name: Hiyas sa Kadayawan
key_events.blurb: A cultural pageant, not a beauty contest, with 11 candidates representing each Davao tribe judged on heritage knowledge and cultural skill.

key_events.name: Dula Kadayawan
key_events.blurb: Traditional tribal games held at People's Park where the 11 communities go head-to-head in indigenous sports.

key_events.name: Madayaw Village
key_events.blurb: A walk-through cultural village with tribal houses, weaving demos, native food stalls, and live ritual performances.

key_events.name: Bantawan Cultural Showcase
key_events.blurb: Nightly tribal music and dance performances at the Bantawan Amphitheater inside Magsaysay Park.

place_intro:
Kadayawan Festival is Davao City's biggest cultural celebration, a thanksgiving feast for a good harvest, the gifts of nature, and the heritage of the eleven ethnolinguistic tribes who have lived in the Davao region for centuries. It happens every third week of August, although the city has stretched the official run to cover the whole month since 2019. The name comes from the Mandaya word madayaw, which roughly translates to good, valuable, or treasured.

The host city, Davao, sits at the southeastern coast of Mindanao. It is the largest city in the Philippines by land area and the regional hub of southern Mindanao, with Mount Apo looming to the southwest and the Davao Gulf to the east. The festival pulls in visitors from all over the country and the wider region, but the heart of the celebration stays firmly local, anchored on the Lumad and Moro communities of the city.

Most of the main programs run in the downtown area, with People's Park, Magsaysay Park, San Pedro Street, and Roxas Avenue serving as the main venues for street dancing, tribal villages, and the floral parade."
tip: Book your Davao accommodation by June at the latest if you want to stay near Roxas Avenue or San Pedro for the peak weekend. Hotels along the parade route sell out fast and rates climb sharply by July. If downtown is full, look at Matina or Bajada and take a Grab in early to catch a good spot before route closures.
what_unique:
What sets Kadayawan apart is its tribal authenticity. It is not a single-tribe affair like the Ati-Atihan, nor a Spanish-era religious fiesta. Kadayawan is built around eleven living indigenous communities (six Lumad tribes and five Moro tribes) who actually live in and around Davao City, and each one has a real stake in the program. The costumes, dances, and rituals you see come straight from the tribes themselves.

The Hiyas sa Kadayawan also makes the festival distinct. Instead of a swimsuit-and-evening-gown pageant, candidates are judged on how well they know their tribe's history, language, weaving, music, and proposed community project. It is a heritage contest dressed up as a pageant.

Another thing that stands out is the focus on Mindanao bounty. Davao is the country's top producer of durian, pomelo, mangosteen, and cacao, and you will see them piled high on the floral floats and sold by the kilo at the harvest markets. The Pamulak parade looks more like a moving farmers market than a typical flower procession."
why_go:
Go for the street dance. The Indak-Indak sa Kadalanan brings dozens of contingents in full tribal regalia from across Mindanao, dancing the entire stretch of San Pedro and Roxas Avenue in choreographed routines that take a full year to prepare. It is one of the largest tribal street dance competitions in the country, and the energy on the route is hard to match.

Go for the food and the fruit. Kadayawan lands right in the middle of durian season, and Magsaysay Park transforms into one giant durian market for the duration of the festival. You can also taste tribal dishes you will not easily find elsewhere, like Bagobo binignit, Maranao palapa, and Tausug tiyula itum, served at the Madayaw Village food stalls.

Go for the culture. The Madayaw Village and Bantawan performances give you a rare chance to meet, talk to, and learn from the tribes themselves. You can watch a Bagobo weaver work an inabal cloth, hear a Maguindanao kulintang ensemble, or sit through a Lumad ritual blessing, all in one afternoon."

facts.label: When
facts.detail: Culminates on July 18, the founding anniversary of South Cotabato province. The full week of events typically runs from July 11 to 18.

facts.label: Where
facts.detail: Alunan Avenue, the South Cotabato Sports Complex, and the provincial capitol grounds host most of the action in the South Cotabato capital.

facts.label: Theme
facts.detail: Centered on the t'nalak, a sacred abaca cloth handwoven by T'boli women of Lake Sebu using patterns said to be revealed in dreams.

facts.label: Edition
facts.detail: First staged in 2000 to mark the founding of South Cotabato province, which was carved out of Cotabato on July 18, 1966.

history.heading: From a young province to the Land of the Dreamweavers
history.body:
South Cotabato was created on July 18, 1966 when it was separated from the old Cotabato province by Republic Act 4849. For decades the province marked its founding day with civic ceremonies, but it had no signature festival that could put it on the national tourism map the way Sinulog did for Cebu or Panagbenga did for Baguio.

In 2000 the provincial government launched the T'nalak Festival as the official anniversary celebration, anchoring it on the t'nalak, the sacred abaca cloth woven by T'boli women in Lake Sebu. T'boli weavers like the late Lang Dulay, a National Living Treasure awardee, had long carried the tradition forward, and provincial leaders wanted the festival to honor that heritage while uniting the tri-people communities of Lumad, Christian, and Muslim residents. The 2025 staging marked the 26th edition of the festival.
history.citation_label: MindaNews coverage of the 26th T'nalak Festival

key_events.name: Civic-Military Grand Parade
key_events.blurb: Opening-day march through Koronadal led by the governor, with a ceremonial gong-striking at the South Cotabato Sports Complex to formally launch the week.

key_events.name: Magsinadya Street Dancing Competition
key_events.blurb: The headline contest of the Tri-People Grand Parade, where municipal contingents in T'boli-inspired costumes dance their way down Alunan Avenue.

key_events.name: Agri-Trade Fair
key_events.blurb: Two-week showcase of products from the province's 10 LGUs, from Tupi pineapples to Lake Sebu tilapia and tribal handicrafts.

key_events.name: Weaving Flavours of the South
key_events.blurb: Cooking showdown split into a Filipino Cuisine Cook-Off and a Grand Kakanin Setup featuring native rice cakes from each town.

key_events.name: Indigenous Peoples Day
key_events.blurb: Day dedicated to T'boli, B'laan, and other Lumad communities, with rituals, traditional music, and a marketplace of handwoven t'nalak.

key_events.name: Mutya ng South Cotabato
key_events.blurb: Provincial beauty pageant where candidates represent the host LGUs and showcase advocacy work tied to weaving heritage.

place_intro:
T'nalak Festival is the annual week-long anniversary celebration of South Cotabato province, with all the big events staged in Koronadal City, the provincial capital. The festival takes its name from the t'nalak, a handwoven abaca cloth created by T'boli women of Lake Sebu using patterns that weavers say come to them in dreams. That is why South Cotabato bills itself as the Land of the Dreamweavers.

Koronadal sits in the SOCCSKSARGEN region of southern Mindanao, a roughly 90-minute drive from General Santos City. During festival week, Alunan Avenue, the South Cotabato Sports Complex, and the capitol grounds turn into one long open-air venue for parades, contests, trade fairs, and night markets.

The celebration is meant to be a tri-people gathering of Lumad, Christian, and Muslim residents. T'boli and B'laan motifs run through every parade, stage backdrop, and souvenir stall, but the festival also makes room for modern crowd-pullers like motocross, live bands, and beauty pageants."
tip: Base yourself in Koronadal but block off one full day for a side trip to Lake Sebu, about an hour away by van. That is where you can actually watch T'boli weavers at work, buy t'nalak straight from the source, and see the patterns the whole festival is built around. Book the lake ziplines in the morning before the afternoon haze rolls in.
what_unique:
No other Philippine fiesta is built around a single piece of textile the way T'nalak Festival is. The t'nalak is not just a costume reference, it is the reason the festival exists. Every parade contingent works the abaca cloth's red, black, and natural ecru palette into their outfits, and the official patterns are based on real T'boli weaving motifs like the bulinglangit and the kabangi.

The festival is also one of the few in Mindanao that is intentionally tri-people. The program deliberately gives floor time to Lumad rituals, Muslim communities from neighboring provinces, and the Christian settler population that arrived after the Koronadal Valley was opened to homesteaders in the 1930s. You see this in the parade order, the food contests, and the cultural night line-up.

Unlike beach-town fiestas, T'nalak runs on highland weather. Koronadal sits on a plateau, so July nights are cool enough that you actually want a light jacket for the evening street parties on Alunan Avenue, a rarity for a Philippine summer festival."
why_go:
Go for the Magsinadya street dance, the festival's headline event. Contingents from all 10 South Cotabato LGUs spend months drilling choreography that reworks T'boli movements into a competition piece, and the costumes are some of the most photographed in any Mindanao festival. The route runs straight down Alunan Avenue, so sidewalk viewing is easy if you arrive by 7 a.m.

Go for the food. The Weaving Flavours of the South cook-off pits chefs from across the province in a Filipino cuisine round and a Grand Kakanin setup, while the night market along Alunan turns into a food crawl of grilled tuna panga from GenSan, Lake Sebu tilapia, native chicken tinola, and durian from the Tupi farms. Trade-fair tents at the capitol grounds let you buy weaving, brass, and beadwork directly from T'boli and B'laan artisans.

Go for the side trips. Koronadal is the natural jump-off for Lake Sebu, Seven Falls, the Tupi pineapple plantations, and Mount Matutum. A lot of DIY travelers build a 4-day loop that pairs two festival days with two highland days, which is hard to do with most other regional fiestas."

facts.label: When
facts.detail: Held annually from Holy Monday through Easter Sunday, with the biggest crowds gathering on Good Friday and Easter morning for the Pugutan.

facts.label: Where
facts.detail: Centered in Boac, the provincial capital, with parallel observances in Gasan, Santa Cruz, Mogpog, and Buenavista across Marinduque island.

facts.label: Star of the Show
facts.detail: Townsfolk dress as Roman centurions in carved wooden masks, helmets, capes, and tunics, roaming the streets in silence to surprise onlookers.

facts.label: Heritage Status
facts.detail: Recognized by the National Commission for Culture and the Arts as one of the country's oldest Lenten folk traditions, dating to the 1800s.

history.heading: From a parish priest's Lenten play to a national icon
history.body:
The Moriones traces back to 1807 in the town of Mogpog, when Spanish Augustinian Recollect parish priest Dionisio Santiago staged a Lenten dramatization of the story of Longinus, the Roman centurion who pierced the side of Christ on the cross. According to local accounts, Father Santiago wanted to deepen the piety of the townsfolk during Holy Week and used the masked centurion costume as a visual hook that ordinary farmers and fishermen could understand. The role of Longinus, the half-blind soldier whose sight was restored by Christ's blood, became the dramatic centerpiece of the week.

The practice spread from Mogpog to Boac, Gasan, and Santa Cruz over the next century, with each town developing its own mask-carving styles and pasos, or scenes. Families began passing down hand-carved wooden masks from grandfather to grandson, and serving as a Moryon became a panata, a personal vow of penance for healing, thanksgiving, or atonement. By the 1960s the Department of Tourism, then under Jose Aspiras, helped promote Moriones as a flagship Philippine festival, and Marinduque was branded the Lenten Capital of the Philippines, a title it still uses today.
history.citation_label: National Commission for Culture and the Arts

key_events.name: Pagsusuot ng Maskara
key_events.blurb: On Holy Monday, devotees who made a panata kneel before their parish priest, get blessed, and put on their wooden masks to begin a week of silent penance.

key_events.name: Via Crucis Procession
key_events.blurb: On Holy Wednesday and Maundy Thursday, masked Moryons walk alongside life-sized santos in the Stations of the Cross procession through Boac's narrow streets.

key_events.name: Antipo at Pugutan
key_events.blurb: On Good Friday and again on Easter Sunday, townsfolk reenact the chase, capture, and beheading of Longinus in an open-air street drama led by Moryon actors.

key_events.name: Putong Welcome Ritual
key_events.blurb: Visitors stepping off the Balanacan port are sometimes greeted with the Putong, a song-and-crown ritual unique to Marinduque that blesses guests with long life.

key_events.name: Bati sa Lansangan
key_events.blurb: On Black Saturday night, parishes hold the Salubong rehearsal where the risen Christ image meets the Virgin Mary, with Moryons standing watch in formation.

key_events.name: Easter Sunday Parade
key_events.blurb: The week closes with a parade of Moryons from every Marinduque town converging in Boac plaza for mask judging, photo ops, and the final unmasking.

place_intro: The Moriones Festival is a week-long Lenten folk drama held every Holy Week in the island province of Marinduque, with Boac, the provincial capital, as its main stage. Locals in carved wooden masks, plumed helmets, capes, and Roman tunics roam the streets in silent character as morions, the centurions who guarded Christ at the cross. The week climaxes in the reenactment of the story of Longinus, the half-blind Roman soldier whose eye was healed by the blood of Christ and who later refused to deny what he had witnessed.\n\nBoac sits on the western coast of Marinduque, a heart-shaped island in the Sibuyan Sea reachable by RORO ferry from Lucena port in Quezon or by a short propeller flight from Manila. The town is built around the 18th-century Boac Cathedral, perched on a small hill overlooking the Boac River, and the cathedral plaza becomes the festival's open-air theater. The whole province takes part, with Mogpog, Gasan, and Santa Cruz running their own parallel pasos and mask-carving traditions, but Boac draws the largest crowds and the most televised coverage."
tip: Book your Lucena to Balanacan RORO ferry tickets at least two weeks before Holy Week and reserve a homestay in Boac poblacion, not in Santa Cruz, if you want walking access to the Good Friday Pugutan and the Easter Sunday parade. Bring cash since Boac has only a handful of ATMs and lines get long.
what_unique: No other Philippine festival is built around a masked silent character the way Moriones is. The morions never speak in costume, they only gesture, chase children, pose for photos, and march. The masks themselves are hand-carved from soft dapdap or santol wood by local artisans in Mogpog and Boac, each one painted with fierce mustachioed European features, and many families have heirloom masks passed down for three or four generations. Serving as a morion is treated as a panata, a religious vow, not a performance gig.\n\nThe festival is also a full-week immersive drama rather than a single parade day. From Holy Monday through Easter Sunday the streets are populated with morions doing errands, eating at carinderias, and napping under acacia trees in full costume. Side rituals layer on top of the centurion story, including the Antipo street chase, the Pugutan beheading reenactment, and the Putong, a Marinduque-only welcome ritual where guests are sung to and crowned with flowers. Marinduque has held the Department of Tourism title Lenten Capital of the Philippines since the 1970s, and the local economy openly leans on the week to carry tourism for the rest of the year."
why_go:
Go for the visual shock of seeing hundreds of masked Roman centurions roaming a small provincial town for an entire week. Unlike fiestas that peak in a single afternoon parade, Moriones unfolds across seven days of street theater, processions, and unscripted encounters. You can share breakfast with a morion eating pandesal, then watch the same person chase children through the plaza an hour later.

Go also for the story. The Longinus narrative is a specifically Marinduque retelling that locals have shaped for over two centuries. Watching the Pugutan, the climactic beheading scene staged on Good Friday and Easter Sunday, you see actual townsfolk who prepared their roles for months as part of a panata. The crowd reacts the way you would at a funeral, not a tourist event.

Finally, Marinduque itself is a reason to come. The island is small enough to circle by tricycle and habal-habal in a weekend, the beaches in Gasan and Torrijos are quiet, and the food leans on fresh seafood and local kalamay rice cake. Two extra days of island time turn the trip into a proper Holy Week getaway."

facts.label: Festival Date
facts.detail: Aligned with the feast day of San Isidro Labrador, patron saint of farmers. The main celebration runs the full day with house judging in the morning and procession in the afternoon.

facts.label: Host Town
facts.detail: A mountainside town at the foot of Mt. Banahaw, about 4 to 5 hours by land from Manila via Lucena or Tayabas. Crowds spill into Lucban Church plaza and the official kiping route.

facts.label: National Treasure
facts.detail: Recognized by the National Commission for Culture and the Arts (NCCA) as a National Cultural Treasure, alongside its sister festival in San Isidro town.

facts.label: Must-Try Food
facts.detail: Served on a banana leaf, no utensils. Pair it with Lucban longganisa, hardinera, and fried kiping dipped in vinegar from any stall along the route.

history.heading: From Mt. Banahaw offerings to a National Cultural Treasure
history.body:
Pahiyas traces its roots to a pre-Hispanic harvest rite. Lucban farmers used to bring portions of their crops to the foothills of Mt. Banahaw, where they offered the produce to ancestor spirits and asked for the next planting season to be just as kind. When Spanish friars arrived in the 16th century, the ritual was absorbed into Catholic practice and the offerings were redirected to San Isidro Labrador, the patron saint of farmers whose feast day is May 15.

As harvests grew, the church grounds could no longer hold all the offerings. Farmers started displaying their produce in front of their own houses, and the parish priest walked from home to home blessing each one. That door to door blessing route is the same template the modern festival follows today.

The contemporary Pahiyas as a competitive, town-wide spectacle was formalized in 1963 by Fernando Cadeliña Nañawa, then president of Lucban's Art Club, who reorganized the displays into a judged route. In 2013, the NCCA declared the Lucban and San Isidro Pahiyas a National Cultural Treasure for safeguarding kiping-making and the harvest thanksgiving tradition.
history.citation_label: Spot.ph: The Story of the Pahiyas Festival

key_events.name: Holy Mass and Procession of San Isidro
key_events.blurb: Morning Mass at Lucban Church, followed by the carriage procession of San Isidro Labrador around the official route past the decorated houses.

key_events.name: Pahiyas House Decoration Judging
key_events.blurb: Homeowners along the route compete with facades dressed in kiping, palay, fruits, and vegetables. Judges score creativity, use of local produce, and craftsmanship.

key_events.name: Kiping Making Demos
key_events.blurb: Families show how rice flour is ground, tinted, steamed on cabal leaves, and sun-dried into the leaf-shaped wafers that define the festival.

key_events.name: Parikitan and Cultural Parade
key_events.blurb: Street dancing, brass bands, and contingents in farmer costumes wind through the town center, usually staged before or after the religious procession.

key_events.name: Mutya ng Lucban
key_events.blurb: Beauty pageant featuring Lucbanin candidates in agriculture-themed costumes. Coronation night is typically held in the days leading up to May 15.

key_events.name: Lucban Food and Pasalubong Trail
key_events.blurb: Stalls and home kitchens along the route serve pancit habhab on banana leaves, Lucban longganisa, hardinera, broas, and fried kiping with vinegar.

place_intro:
Pahiyas is the harvest thanksgiving fiesta of Lucban, Quezon, held every May 15 in honor of San Isidro Labrador, the patron saint of Filipino farmers. The whole town becomes the venue. Households along a chosen route dress up their facades from roof to sidewalk using kiping (leaf-shaped rice wafers in food-colored pastels), palay, corn, fresh fruits, vegetables, and woven hats called sambalilo. Judges pass through in the morning and award prizes for the most striking display, and a religious procession follows in the afternoon.

Lucban sits at the foot of Mt. Banahaw, about 4 to 5 hours by land from Manila via Lucena or Tayabas. It is a small town the rest of the year, which is part of why Pahiyas feels personal. You are walking past actual living rooms and front gates, not a fenced off festival ground. Locals will hand you fried kiping and motion you to come closer and take a photo of their facade.

The festival was declared a National Cultural Treasure by the NCCA in 2013, recognizing the kiping craft and the centuries-old harvest rite behind it."
tip: Walk the route in the opposite direction of the procession. Start at the tail end of the official route by 7 to 8 AM, before the judges and the noon crowd hit. You get clean photos of the kiping facades without the back of someone's head, and you finish near Lucban Church just in time for the Mass and the first batch of pancit habhab off the wok.
what_unique:
Other Philippine festivals are built around dance, costume, or pageantry. Pahiyas is built around food you can actually eat off the wall. The kiping that covers every facade is real rice dough, steamed on cabal or kabal leaves, sun-dried, and tinted in pinks, greens, and yellows. After the procession passes, families take the kiping down, fry it crisp, and serve it with vinegar to anyone walking by. Nothing is wasted.

The second thing that sets Pahiyas apart is that the festival happens on private homes, not on a stage. Each household designs its own facade, sources its own produce, and competes against the neighbors for bragging rights.
